Located on the [Durham Coast](/wiki/Durham_Coast "Durham Coast"), Seaham is situated 6 miles (10 kilometres) south of [Sunderland](/wiki/Sunderland "Sunderland") and 13 miles (21 km) east of [Durham](/wiki/Durham,_England "Durham, England"). The town grew from the late 19th century onwards as a result of investments in its harbour and coal mines. The town is twinned with the German town of [Gerlingen](/wiki/Gerlingen "Gerlingen").

**Seaham** ([/ˈsiːəm/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") [*SEE-əm*](/wiki/Help:Pronunciation_respelling_key "Help:Pronunciation respelling key")[[3]](#cite_note-3)) is a seaside town in [County Durham](/wiki/County_Durham_(district) "County Durham (district)"), [England](/wiki/England "England"). Located on the [Durham Coast](/wiki/Durham_Coast "Durham Coast"), Seaham is situated 6 miles (10 kilometres) south of [Sunderland](/wiki/Sunderland "Sunderland") and 13 miles (21 km) east of [Durham](/wiki/Durham,_England "Durham, England"). The town grew from the late 19th century onwards as a result of investments in its harbour and coal mines. The town is twinned with the German town of [Gerlingen](/wiki/Gerlingen "Gerlingen").

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Seaham&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

The original village of Seaham has all but vanished; it lay between [St Mary's Church](/wiki/St_Mary%27s_Church,_Seaham "St Mary's Church, Seaham") and [Seaham Hall](/wiki/Seaham_Hall "Seaham Hall") (i.e. somewhat to the north of the current town centre).[[4]](#cite_note-4) The parish church, St Mary the Virgin, has a late 7th century. The Anglian [nave](/wiki/Nave "Nave") resembles the [church](/wiki/Escomb_Church "Escomb Church") at [Escomb](/wiki/Escomb "Escomb") in many respects.[[5]](#cite_note-CoE-5)

Until the early years of the 19th century, Seaham was a small rural agricultural farming community whose only claim to fame was that the local landowner's daughter, [Anne Isabella Milbanke](/wiki/Anne_Isabella_Milbanke "Anne Isabella Milbanke"), was married at Seaham Hall to [Lord Byron](/wiki/Lord_Byron "Lord Byron"), on 2 January 1815.[[6]](#cite_note-6)
Byron began writing his *Hebrew Melodies* at Seaham and they were published in April 1815.
It would seem that Byron was bored in wintry Seaham, though the sea enthralled him.
As he wrote in a letter to a friend:

> Upon this dreary coast we have nothing but county meetings and shipwrecks; and I have this day dined upon fish, which probably dined upon the crews of several colliers lost in the late gales. But I saw the sea once more in all the glories of surf and foam.

The marriage was short-lived, producing as its only child the [mathematician](/wiki/Mathematician "Mathematician") [Ada Lovelace](/wiki/Ada_Lovelace "Ada Lovelace"), but it was long enough to have been a drain on the Milbanke estate. The area's fortunes changed when the Milbankes sold out in 1821 to the [3rd Marquess of Londonderry](/wiki/Charles_William_Vane,_3rd_Marquess_of_Londonderry "Charles William Vane, 3rd Marquess of Londonderry"),[[7]](#cite_note-7) who built a harbour, in 1828, to facilitate transport of goods from locally encouraged industries (the first coal mine was begun in 1845). However, this harbour later proved inadequate to deal with the millions of tonnes of coal and the [6th Marquess](/wiki/Charles_Stewart_Vane-Tempest-Stewart,_6th_Marquess_of_Londonderry "Charles Stewart Vane-Tempest-Stewart, 6th Marquess of Londonderry") commissioned engineers [Patrick Meik](/wiki/Patrick_Meik "Patrick Meik") and [Charles Meik](/wiki/Charles_Meik "Charles Meik") to reclaim land and extend and deepen the dock. It was officially opened in 1905. The harbour is of particular interest because it consists of a series of interconnecting locks, rather than the more typical two wall construction.

As early as 1823, the 3rd Marquess had approached the architect [John Dobson](/wiki/John_Dobson_(architect) "John Dobson (architect)") with a view to his drawing up plans for a town to be built around the harbour. Dobson did so, but the planned approach foundered for lack of funds, and the town instead grew in a more piecemeal fashion.[[8]](#cite_note-STC-8) To begin with, the town was itself called Seaham Harbour (to differentiate it from the ancient village); in time, though, the settlement as a whole came to be known as Seaham.

In 1853 John Candlish built the Londonderry Bottleworks in the town. It was the largest glass bottle works in Britain and survived until 1921. Candlish went on to become mayor and, in 1868, Liberal MP for [Sunderland](/wiki/Sunderland_(UK_Parliament_constituency) "Sunderland (UK Parliament constituency)"). Waste glass from the bottleworks was dumped at sea and is now washed up as glass pebbles, known as [sea glass](/wiki/Sea_glass "Sea glass"), on local beaches.[[9]](#cite_note-9)[[10]](#cite_note-10)[[11]](#cite_note-11)

In 1928, production started at the last town colliery to be opened, Vane Tempest. By 1992, however, all three pits ([Dawdon Colliery](/wiki/Dawdon#Dawdon_Colliery "Dawdon"), Vane Tempest Colliery and [Seaham Colliery](/wiki/Seaham_Colliery "Seaham Colliery") – known locally as "the Knack") had closed, a process accelerated by the [British miners' strike](/wiki/1984%E2%80%931985_United_Kingdom_miners%27_strike "1984–1985 United Kingdom miners' strike"). The pit closures hit the local economy extremely hard.

Seaham Colliery suffered an underground explosion in 1880 which resulted in the loss of over 160 lives,[[12]](#cite_note-12) including surface workers and rescuers.

Many local families were affected by the tragic loss of eight men and one boy in the 'Seaham Lifeboat Disaster', when the [RNLI](/wiki/RNLI "RNLI") [lifeboat](/wiki/Lifeboat_(rescue) "Lifeboat (rescue)"), the *George Elmy*, foundered on 17 November 1962. To commemorate the event, the new coast road was named George Elmy Lifeboat Way.[[13]](#cite_note-13)

## Governance and politics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Seaham&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Governance and politics")]

An [electoral ward](/wiki/Wards_and_electoral_divisions_of_the_United_Kingdom "Wards and electoral divisions of the United Kingdom") with the same name exists. The population of this ward taken at the 2011 census was 8419.[[1]](#cite_note-OoI-1)

Seaham is part of the [Easington parliament constituency](/wiki/Easington_(UK_Parliament_constituency) "Easington (UK Parliament constituency)") and is currently represented in the [House of Commons](/wiki/House_of_Commons_of_the_United_Kingdom "House of Commons of the United Kingdom") of the [Parliament of the United Kingdom](/wiki/Parliament_of_the_United_Kingdom "Parliament of the United Kingdom") by [Labour](/wiki/Labour_Party_(UK) "Labour Party (UK)") [Member of Parliament](/wiki/Member_of_Parliament_(United_Kingdom) "Member of Parliament (United Kingdom)"), [Grahame Morris](/wiki/Grahame_Morris "Grahame Morris"), who has served since the [2010 general election](/wiki/2010_United_Kingdom_general_election "2010 United Kingdom general election").

## Education

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Seaham&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Education")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a1/Seaham_coastline_-_geograph.org.uk_-_624314.jpg/250px-Seaham_coastline_-_geograph.org.uk_-_624314.jpg)](/wiki/File:Seaham_coastline_-_geograph.org.uk_-_624314.jpg)

The coast at almost the northernmost point of Seaham, looking towards Sunderland

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0f/Church_Street%2C_Seaham_-_geograph.org.uk_-_1529671.jpg/250px-Church_Street%2C_Seaham_-_geograph.org.uk_-_1529671.jpg)](/wiki/File:Church_Street,_Seaham_-_geograph.org.uk_-_1529671.jpg)

Church Street, Seaham town centre

Seaham has one secondary school, without a sixth-form, called [Seaham High School](/wiki/Seaham_High_School "Seaham High School") (before 2016 known as Seaham School of Technology).[[14]](#cite_note-14)

## Sport

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Seaham&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Sport")]

Seaham's main football team is [Seaham Red Star F.C.](/wiki/Seaham_Red_Star_F.C. "Seaham Red Star F.C."), formerly Seaham Colliery Welfare Red Star, located near Seaham's Red Star park. The club plays in [Northern League Division Two](/wiki/Northern_League_Division_Two "Northern League Division Two").

Seaham has three cricket clubs, Seaham Harbour Cricket Club, Seaham Park Cricket Club and Dawdon Welfare Cricket Club.

In the 2019-20 rugby season, Seaham RUFC were promoted from [Durham/Northumberland 3](/wiki/Durham/Northumberland_3 "Durham/Northumberland 3") into [Durham/Northumberland 2](/wiki/Durham/Northumberland_2 "Durham/Northumberland 2"). The side won the Durham County Junior Cup
in 2023 and 2025.

## Media coverage

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Seaham&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Media coverage")]

The final scene of the 1971 film, *[Get Carter](/wiki/Get_Carter "Get Carter")*, was shot at Blackhall Rocks beach, which is down the coast from Seaham.[[15]](#cite_note-15)[[16]](#cite_note-16)

The rich mining history of the town was highlighted in the 2000 film *[Billy Elliot](/wiki/Billy_Elliot "Billy Elliot")*, which was set during the [1984–1985 United Kingdom miners' strike](/wiki/1984%E2%80%931985_United_Kingdom_miners%27_strike "1984–1985 United Kingdom miners' strike") in the fictional County Durham town of Everington but which displayed characteristics particular to East Durham pit communities such as Seaham and [Easington Colliery](/wiki/Easington_Colliery "Easington Colliery").
Both towns feature as locations in the film, notably [Dawdon](/wiki/Dawdon "Dawdon") Miners' Club, into which Elliot's dad runs when he learns his son has won an audition at dance school.
Elliot's "angry dance" scene takes place in Dawdon between Embleton Street and Stavordale Street West.

The opening scene in *[Alien 3](/wiki/Alien_3 "Alien 3")* (1992) was filmed on Blast Beach, at [Dawdon](/wiki/Dawdon "Dawdon").[[17]](#cite_note-17)
The town has also served as a location for the [BAFTA](/wiki/BAFTA "BAFTA") nominated film *Life For Ruth* (1962) starring [Janet Munro](/wiki/Janet_Munro "Janet Munro") and [Patrick McGoohan](/wiki/Patrick_McGoohan "Patrick McGoohan").[[18]](#cite_note-18)

The town appeared in the [BBC Three](/wiki/BBC_Three "BBC Three") sitcom *[Live!Girls! present Dogtown](/wiki/Live!Girls!_present_Dogtown "Live!Girls! present Dogtown")* which premiered on the channel in autumn 2006. According to the *[Sunderland Echo](/wiki/Sunderland_Echo "Sunderland Echo")* (11 February 1999), scenes from *[Saving Private Ryan](/wiki/Saving_Private_Ryan "Saving Private Ryan")* (1998) were also going to be filmed in Seaham, but government intervention moved production elsewhere.

According to Tom McNee's 1992 portrait of the town *The Changing Face of Seaham: 1928–1992*, [St. John](/wiki/John_the_Evangelist "John the Evangelist")'s parish church was used as the setting of a 1985 service recorded for [BBC Radio 3](/wiki/BBC_Radio_3 "BBC Radio 3"). Also, a two-part [Channel 4](/wiki/Channel_4 "Channel 4") documentary profiled the town in 1991.

## Landmarks

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Seaham&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Landmarks")]

To the south, beside the road to Dalton-le-Dale, are the remains of Dalden Tower, comprising the ruins of a 16th-century tower and fragments of later buildings.

The harbour itself may be said to be the principal landmark of the nineteenth-century town; though the Londonderry Institute in Tempest Road (1853-5 by [Thomas Oliver](/wiki/Thomas_Oliver_(architect) "Thomas Oliver (architect)")) with its monumental Greek-style [portico](/wiki/Portico "Portico")[[19]](#cite_note-19) provides something of a glimpse of the Marquess's original vision for the town.
Of a slightly later date, the former Londonderry Offices on the sea front once served as headquarters for the mining and other businesses of the Londonderry family.
A statue of the 6th Marquess stands in the forecourt.
Also dating from an early stage in the town's development is the town-centre church of St John, Seaham Harbour (1835–40).
For the very much older St Mary's, Seaham, and its neighbour Seaham Hall, see above.

For just over a hundred years the harbour was towered over by a 58 ft (18 m) lighthouse on Red Acre Point, immediately to the north, designed by [William Chapman](/wiki/William_Chapman_(engineer) "William Chapman (engineer)"). Erected in 1835, it displayed a fixed white light above a revolving red light (an unusual configuration, provided so as to distinguish it from the north pier light at Sunderland);[[20]](#cite_note-RC1861-20) both lights were displayed from the same tower, the upper being 100 feet (30 m) and the lower 54 feet (16 m) above mean sea level.[[21]](#cite_note-Norrie1846-21) The lighthouse was gas-lit, with an arrangement of [third-order](/wiki/Third_order_Fresnel_lens "Third order Fresnel lens") [catadioptric](/wiki/Catadioptric "Catadioptric") lenses provided by [Chance Brothers & Co.](/wiki/Chance_Brothers "Chance Brothers")[[20]](#cite_note-RC1861-20) It was decommissioned in 1905, when the harbour was expanded and the current black-and-white striped pier-head light was constructed. Red Acre lighthouse was left standing, however, to serve as a [daymark](/wiki/Daymark "Daymark"), until 1940 when the whole structure was swiftly demolished in case it should serve to assist enemy navigators.[[22]](#cite_note-22)

A steel statue, *1101* (locally also known as *[Tommy](/wiki/Tommy_(statue) "Tommy (statue)")*) by local artist [Ray Lonsdale](/wiki/Ray_Lonsdale "Ray Lonsdale"), commemorating World War One and initially erected temporarily for three months, was the subject of a local fund-raising drive in 2014 to retain it on the town's seafront.[[23]](#cite_note-BBCTommy-23)

The open area at the north end of Seaham on the B1287 adjacent to the Seaham Hall Beach Car Park has been designated a *Mole Sanctuary*[[24]](#cite_note-MoleSanctuary-24) with a plaque stating "Famous Moles" added to the carpark road sign. The history of this designation is uncertain though a high number of mole hills are observable.

Between 1929 and 1935, the Member of Parliament (MP) for [Seaham](/wiki/Seaham_(UK_Parliament_constituency) "Seaham (UK Parliament constituency)") (the defunct constituency which covered the area now renamed Easington) was Labour Prime Minister [Ramsay MacDonald](/wiki/Ramsay_MacDonald "Ramsay MacDonald").
Easington constituency has only ever returned [Labour](/wiki/Labour_Party_(UK) "Labour Party (UK)") candidates to Parliament,[[25]](#cite_note-25)
and at the [2010 General Election](/wiki/2010_United_Kingdom_general_election "2010 United Kingdom general election"), Labour candidate [Grahame Morris](/wiki/Grahame_Morris "Grahame Morris") was elected with a majority of 14,982[[26]](#cite_note-26) votes.

Seaham has also produced several able footballers, some of whom have gone on to play for the local team, [Sunderland](/wiki/Sunderland_A.F.C. "Sunderland A.F.C."), such as [Richie Pitt](/wiki/Richie_Pitt "Richie Pitt") and [Gary Rowell](/wiki/Gary_Rowell "Gary Rowell"). [Terry Fenwick](/wiki/Terry_Fenwick "Terry Fenwick") and [Brian Marwood](/wiki/Brian_Marwood "Brian Marwood") played for England, with the latter, on retirement from football, working as a commentator for [Sky Sports](/wiki/Sky_Sports "Sky Sports").
[Paul Gascoigne](/wiki/Paul_Gascoigne "Paul Gascoigne") also lived in Seaham in the late 1990s, while playing for [Middlesbrough](/wiki/Middlesbrough_F.C. "Middlesbrough F.C.").

Other notable residents include:

* [Baritone](/wiki/Baritone "Baritone") Sir [Thomas Allen](/wiki/Thomas_Allen_(baritone) "Thomas Allen (baritone)") was born in Seaham in 1944
* [Martin Brammer](/wiki/Martin_Brammer "Martin Brammer"), musician
* [Bob Fox](/wiki/Bob_Fox_(musician) "Bob Fox (musician)"), musician
* [Elizabeth Sterling Haynes](/wiki/Elizabeth_Sterling_Haynes "Elizabeth Sterling Haynes") (born in Seaham in 1897), Canadian theatre activist
* [Janie Jones](/wiki/Janie_Jones "Janie Jones"), singer
* [William McNally](/wiki/William_McNally "William McNally"), Victoria Cross winner
* [Ian Pattison](/wiki/Ian_Pattison_(cricketer) "Ian Pattison (cricketer)"), cricketer
* [John Stapylton Grey Pemberton](/wiki/John_Stapylton_Grey_Pemberton "John Stapylton Grey Pemberton"), MP for [Sunderland](/wiki/Sunderland "Sunderland"), who lived at Hawthorn Tower, Seaham Harbour[[27]](#cite_note-27)
* [Agony aunt](/wiki/Agony_aunt "Agony aunt") and author [Denise Robertson](/wiki/Denise_Robertson_(presenter) "Denise Robertson (presenter)") lived in the town for many years
* [Alex Russell](/wiki/Alex_Russell_(footballer,_born_1944) "Alex Russell (footballer, born 1944)"), former professional footballer
* [Peter Willey](/wiki/Peter_Willey "Peter Willey"), Northamptonshire and England cricketer, went to Seaham Secondary School
